document:
Linenoise works by taking direct control of terminal input while an edit session is active. Its implementation keeps a copy of the original terminal settings and switches the terminal into raw mode so individual keypresses can be read and handled immediately rather than waiting for canonical line-buffered input. The edit state records the input and output file descriptors in `struct linenoiseState`, allowing both blocking and nonblocking APIs to share the same core input machinery.

The main input step occurs in `linenoiseEditFeed`. It reads one byte from the input file descriptor. If the read would block in nonblocking mode, the function returns the special sentinel `linenoiseEditMore`, meaning editing should continue and the caller should wait for more input. If the read reaches end-of-file or a terminating control sequence, the function returns `NULL` or a completed line depending on the event. This design lets the blocking API call the feed function repeatedly, while async applications can call it only when their event loop says input is available.

Raw input is interpreted as a mixture of ordinary bytes, control characters, and escape sequences. Enter completes the line and returns a copy of the current buffer. Ctrl-C aborts by returning `NULL` with an error-style condition. Ctrl-D either deletes the character at the cursor or, if the buffer is empty, behaves like end-of-file. Backspace deletes the character before the cursor. Ctrl-A and Ctrl-E move to the beginning and end of the line; Ctrl-B and Ctrl-F move left and right; Ctrl-P and Ctrl-N navigate history; Ctrl-L clears the screen; Ctrl-U, Ctrl-K, and Ctrl-W delete larger spans of text.

Arrow keys and other navigation keys arrive as escape sequences. Linenoise reads the bytes after Escape and handles common `ESC [` and `ESC O` patterns. Up and Down map to previous and next history entries, Left and Right move the cursor, Home and End jump to the start or end of the line, and Delete can remove the character under the cursor. The implementation intentionally relies on a limited set of terminal escape sequences to stay small and broadly compatible.

UTF-8 input is handled by detecting the byte length of a multibyte character, reading the remaining bytes, and inserting the full sequence as one logical character. Cursor movement and deletion use helper functions that move by UTF-8 character or grapheme cluster rather than simply moving by one byte. This matters because a visible character can occupy multiple bytes, and some display forms such as combining marks, emoji sequences, and wide characters affect terminal column width differently.

Linenoise also handles unsupported or unusual terminal situations. If the input file descriptor is not a TTY, it can fall back to a non-terminal path. It has a small list of unsupported terminal names such as dumb terminals, and it can also use an environment override for TTY assumptions. Raw terminal input is therefore the layer that makes linenoise interactive: it converts low-level byte reads and terminal control sequences into editing commands over the current line buffer.
